html:
XXXII

Early one morning Pelle wandered into the city. He had risen before Ellen, in order to avoid the painfulness of sitting down to breakfast with her. Ellen tried all sorts of ruses in order to give him a proper breakfast, and it was not difficult to persuade his stomach; but afterward he felt ashamed that he should have been cared for at the cost of others; and cunning though he was too, he could not get the better of her save by slipping away while she was still asleep.

His fasting condition endowed the city, and the whole of life, with a curiously unsubstantial aspect. Before him lay a long day full of terrific labors, and behind him was the fresh triumph of the day before.

As matters now stood, the employers in the iron industry had conceived the cunning idea of founding a blackleg Union for smiths and mechanics, and of giving it a name closely resembling that of the genuine Union. Then they sent circulars to the men, stating that work would be resumed on the following day. Many of the men were not accustomed to read, and regarded the circular as an order from their own Union, while others were enticed by the high wages offered by the new society. There was great confusion among the workers of these trades. As soon as the trick was exposed every respectable man drew back; but there was a great deal of disappointment, and they felt horribly ashamed before their comrades.

Pelle was furious at this trick, which affected him more especially, as the leader in open battle; he had suffered a defeat, and he meditated revenge. In spite of all the efforts of the pickets, it was not possible to procure a full list of the strikebreakers; his chagrin on this account burned in his heart, like a shameful sense of impotency; hitherto he had been noted for getting to the bottom of anything he undertook! He resolved then and there to meet ruse with ruse. He set a trap for his opponents, so that they themselves should deliver the strikebreakers into his hands. One morning he published his list in The Working Man with the proud remark, ‚ÄúLook, the enemy has no more!‚Äù Did the employers really fall into the trap, or was the fate of the strikebreakers really indifferent to them? Next morning their organ protested, and gave the number of the blacklegs and their names into the bargain!

This was a smack! A good one this; it brought a light to the thin, impassive faces. There was an answer to the trick of the other day! This Pelle was a deuce of a fellow! Three cheers for ‚ÄúLightning Pelle!‚Äù Hip, hip, hurrah!

Pelle was the deuce of a fellow as he strode along ruddy and full of pugnacity, with the echoes from the side-streets and the tenement-houses mingled with his own vigorous footsteps. Streets and houses were white with the night‚Äôs hoar frost, and overhead the air was full of a peculiar glow that came from the city‚ÅÝ‚Äîa light flowing from hidden sources. He had left all his cares at home; on every hand working-folk were greeting him, and his greeting in return was like an inspiriting song. He did not know them, but they knew him! The feeling that his work‚ÅÝ‚Äîhowever deep the scars it might leave‚ÅÝ‚Äîwas arousing gratitude, had an uplifting effect upon him.

The city was in its morning mood. The lockout lay like a paralyzing hand upon everything; business was slack, and the middle classes were complaining, but there was no prospect of peace; both sides were irreconcilable. The workers had lost nothing through the rash cessation of the masons. Sympathy for the lower classes had become a political principle; and contributions were still pouring in from the country. Considerable sums came from abroad. The campaign was now costing the workers half a million kroner a week; and the help from outside was like a drop in the ocean. But it had the effect of a moral support, and it stimulated the self-taxation to which all were subject. The hundred thousand households of the poor parted with their last possessions in order to continue the struggle; they meant to force a decision that should affect their whole future. The employers tried to hinder the great National Federation by calling the attention of the authorities to an ancient statute concerning mendicancy; but that merely aroused merriment. A little laughter over such expedients was permissible.

The workers had become accustomed to starvation. They went no more into the forest, but strolled thoughtfully through the streets like people who have too much time on their hands, so that the city‚Äôs face wore a peculiar stamp of meditative poverty. Their loitering steps aroused no echo, and in the houses the quietness gave one food for reflection. The noisy, ever-hungry children were scattered over the face of the country‚ÅÝ‚Äîthey at least had plenty to eat. But the place was empty for the lack of them!

Pelle met several squads of workers; they were on the way to the various roll-calls. They raised their heads as he passed; his footsteps echoed loudly enough for all! It was the hope and the will of forty thousand men that passed there‚ÅÝ‚ÄîPelle was the expression of them all. They stared at his indomitable figure, and drew themselves up. ‚ÄúA devil of a chap!‚Äù they told one another joyfully; ‚Äúhe looks as if he could trample ‚Äôem all underfoot! Look at him‚ÅÝ‚Äîhe scarcely makes way for that great loaded wagon! Long live Pelle, boys!‚Äù

The tavern-keepers stood on their cellar stairs gaping up at the morning sky‚ÅÝ‚Äîthis was a time of famine for them! In the tavern windows hung cards with the inscription: ‚ÄúContributions received here for the locked-out workers!‚Äù

On the Queen Luise Bridge Pelle encountered a pale, fat little man in a shabby coat. He had flabby features and a great red nose. ‚ÄúGood morning, General!‚Äù cried Pelle gaily; the man made a condescending movement with his hand. This was The Working Man‚Äôs man of straw; a sometime capitalist, who for a small weekly wage was, as far as the public was concerned, the responsible editor of the paper. He served various terms of imprisonment for the paper, and for a further payment of five kroner a week he also worked out in prison the fines inflicted on the paper. When he was not in jail he kept himself alive by drinking. He suffered from megalomania, and considered that he led the whole labor movement; for which reason he could not bear Pelle.

In the great courtyard of The Working Man building the dockers were assembled to answer the roll. The president of their Union met Pelle in the doorway; he was the very man whom Pelle and Howling Peter had rescued down by the harbor‚ÅÝ‚Äînow he was working for the new ideas!

‚ÄúWell, how goes it?‚Äù asked Pelle, shaking his hand.

‚ÄúSplendid! A thousand men all but seven!‚Äù

‚ÄúBut where‚Äôs the joyful Jacob? Is he ill?‚Äù

‚ÄúHe‚Äôs in jail,‚Äù replied the other gloomily. ‚ÄúHe couldn‚Äôt bear to see his old folks starving‚ÅÝ‚Äîso he broke into a grocery, he and his brother‚ÅÝ‚Äîand now they‚Äôre both in prison.‚Äù

For a moment the lines on Pelle‚Äôs forehead were terribly deep and gloomy; he stood gazing blindly into space; the radiant expression left his countenance, which was filled with a pitying gravity. The docker stared at him‚ÅÝ‚Äîwas he going to sleep on his feet? But then he pulled himself together.

‚ÄúWell, comrades, are you finding the days too long?‚Äù he cried gaily.

‚ÄúAch, as for that! It‚Äôs the first time one‚Äôs had the time to get to know one‚Äôs own wife and children properly!‚Äù they replied. ‚ÄúBut for all that it would be fine to get busy again!‚Äù

It was obvious that idleness was at last beginning to depress them; there was a peculiar pondering expression on their impassive features, and their eyes turned to him with a persistent questioning. They asked that this undertaking of his should be settled one way or the other. They were not weakening; they always voted for the continuance of the campaign, for that which they sought depended thereon; but they gazed into his face for a look that might promise success.

He had to answer many singular questions; privation engendered in the most fantastic ideas, which revealed the fact that their quiet, controlled bearing was the product of the observation and the energy of the many.

‚ÄúShall we deprive the rich of all their wealth and power?‚Äù asked one man, after long pondering and gazing at Pelle. The struggle seemed to have dealt hardly with him; but it had lit a spark in his eyes.

‚ÄúYes, we are going now to take our rights as men, and we shall demand that the worker shall be respected,‚Äù Pelle replied. ‚ÄúThen there‚Äôll be no more talk of poor man and gentleman!‚Äù

‚ÄúBut suppose they try to get on top of us again? We must make short work of them, so that they can‚Äôt clamber on our backs and ride us again.‚Äù

‚ÄúDo you want to drive them all onto the Common and shoot them? That‚Äôs not necessary,‚Äù said his neighbor. ‚ÄúWhen this is settled no one will dare to take the food out of our mouths again.‚Äù

‚ÄúWon‚Äôt there be any more poverty then?‚Äù asked the first speaker, turning to Pelle.

‚ÄúNo, once we get our affairs properly in going order; then there will be comfort in every home. Don‚Äôt you read your paper?‚Äù

Yes, he read it, but there was no harm in hearing the great news confirmed by Pelle himself. And Pelle could confirm it, because he never harbored a doubt. It had been difficult to get the masses to grasp the new conception of things‚ÅÝ‚Äîas difficult as to move the earth! Something big must happen in return!

A few of the men had brought out sandwiches and began to eat them as they debated. ‚ÄúGood digestion!‚Äù said Pelle, nodding farewell to them. His mouth was watering, and he remembered that he had had nothing to eat or drink. But he had no time to think about it; he must go to Stolpe to arrange about the posting of the pickets.

Over the way stood Marie in a white cap, with a basket over her arm; she nodded to him, with rosy cheeks. Transplantation had made her grow; every time he saw her she was more erect and prettier.

At his parents‚Äô-in-law the strictest economy prevailed. All sorts of things‚ÅÝ‚Äîhousehold possessions‚ÅÝ‚Äîhad disappeared from that once so comfortable home; but there was no lack of good spirits. Stolpe was pottering about waiting for his breakfast; he had been at work early that morning.

‚ÄúWhat‚Äôs the girl doing?‚Äù he asked. ‚ÄúWe never see her now.‚Äù

‚ÄúShe has such a lot to do,‚Äù said Pelle apologetically. ‚ÄúAnd now she‚Äôs going out to work as well.‚Äù

‚ÄúWell, well, with things as they are she‚Äôs not too fine to lend a hand. But we don‚Äôt really know what‚Äôs amiss with her‚ÅÝ‚Äîshe‚Äôs a rebellious nature! Thank God she‚Äôs not a man‚ÅÝ‚Äîshe would have brought dissolution into the ranks!‚Äù

Breakfast consisted of a portion of coffee and bread-and-butter and porridge. Madam Stolpe could not find her fine new silver coffee-service, which her children had given her on her silver-wedding day. ‚ÄúI must have put it away,‚Äù she said.

‚ÄúWell, well, that‚Äôll soon be found again, mother!‚Äù said Stolpe. ‚ÄúNow we shall soon have better times; many fine things will make their appearance again then, we shall see!‚Äù

‚ÄúHave you been to the machine-works this morning, father-in-law?‚Äù asked Pelle.

‚ÄúYes, I‚Äôve been there. But there is nothing more for the pickets to do. The employers have quartered all the men in the factory; they get full board and all there. There must be a crowd of foreign strikebreakers there‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe work‚Äôs in full swing.‚Äù

This was an overwhelming piece of news! The iron-masters had won the first victory! This would quickly have a most depressing effect on the workers, when they saw that their trade could be kept going without them.

‚ÄúWe must put a bridle on them,‚Äù said Pelle, ‚Äúor they‚Äôll get off the course and the whole organization will fall to pieces. As for those fellows in there, we must get a louse under their shirts somehow.‚Äù

‚ÄúHow can we do that when they are locked in, and the police are patrolling day and night in front of the gates? We can‚Äôt even speak to them.‚Äù Stolpe laughed despairingly.

‚ÄúThen someone must slink in and pretend he‚Äôs in want of employment!‚Äù

Stolpe started. ‚ÄúAs a strikebreaker? You‚Äôll never in this life get a respectable man to do that, even if it‚Äôs only in jest! I wouldn‚Äôt do it myself! A strikebreaker is a strikebreaker, turn and twist it how you will.‚Äù

‚ÄúA strikebreaker, I suppose, is one who does his comrades harm. The man who risks his skin in this way deserves another name.‚Äù

‚ÄúI won‚Äôt admit that,‚Äù said Stolpe. ‚ÄúThat‚Äôs a little too abstract for me; anyhow, I‚Äôm not going to argue with you. But in my catechism it says that he is a strikebreaker who accepts employment where assistance is forbidden‚ÅÝ‚Äîand that I stick to!‚Äù

Pelle might talk as much as he liked; the old man would not budge an inch. ‚ÄúBut it would be another matter if you wanted to do it yourself,‚Äù said Stolpe. ‚ÄúYou don‚Äôt have to account to anyone for what you do‚ÅÝ‚Äîyou just do what comes into your head.‚Äù

‚ÄúI have to account to the Cause for my doings,‚Äù said Pelle sharply, ‚Äúand for that very reason I want to do it myself!‚Äù

Stolpe contracted his arms and stretched them out again. ‚ÄúAh, it would be good to have work again!‚Äù he cried suddenly. ‚ÄúIdleness eats into one‚Äôs limbs like the gout. And now there‚Äôs the rent, mother‚ÅÝ‚Äîwhere the devil are we to get that? It must be paid on the nail on Saturday, otherwise out we go‚ÅÝ‚Äîso the landlord says.‚Äù

‚ÄúWe‚Äôll soon find that, father!‚Äù said Madam Stolpe. ‚ÄúDon‚Äôt you lose heart!‚Äù

Stolpe looked round the room. ‚ÄúYes, there‚Äôs still a bit to take, as Hunger said when he began on the bowels. But listen, Pelle‚ÅÝ‚Äîdo you know what? I‚Äôm your father-in-law-to be sure‚ÅÝ‚Äîbut you haven‚Äôt a wife like mine!‚Äù

‚ÄúI‚Äôm contented with Ellen as she is,‚Äù said Pelle.

There was a knock; it was Stolpe‚Äôs brother, the carpenter. He looked exhausted; he was thin and poorly dressed; his eyes were surrounded by red patches. He did not look at those whose hands he took.

‚ÄúSit down, brother,‚Äù said Stolpe, pushing a chair toward him.

‚ÄúThanks‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI must go on again directly. It was‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI only wanted to tell you‚ÅÝ‚Äîwell.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù He stared out of the window.

‚ÄúIs anything wrong at home?‚Äù

‚ÄúNo, no, not that exactly. I just wanted to say‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI want to give notice that I‚Äôm deserting!‚Äù he cried suddenly.

Stolpe sprang to his feet; he was as white as chalk. ‚ÄúYou think what you are doing!‚Äù he cried threateningly.

‚ÄúI‚Äôve had time enough to think. They are starving, I tell you‚ÅÝ‚Äîand there‚Äôs got to be an end of it. I only wanted to tell you beforehand so that you shouldn‚Äôt hear it from others‚ÅÝ‚Äîafter all, you‚Äôre my brother.‚Äù

‚ÄúYour brother‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI‚Äôm your brother no longer! You do this and we‚Äôve done with one another!‚Äù roared Stolpe, striking the table. ‚ÄúBut you won‚Äôt do it, you shan‚Äôt do it! God damn me, I couldn‚Äôt live through the shame of seeing the comrades condemning my own brother in the open street! And I shall be with them! I shall be the first to give you a kick, if you are my brother!‚Äù He was quite beside himself.

‚ÄúWell, well, we can still talk it over,‚Äù said the carpenter quietly. ‚ÄúBut now you know‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI didn‚Äôt want to do anything behind your back.‚Äù And then he went.

Stolpe paced up and down the room, moving from one object to another. He picked them up and put them down again, quite unthinkingly. His hands were trembling violently; and finally he went to the other room and shut himself in. After a time his wife entered the room. ‚ÄúYou had better go, Pelle! I don‚Äôt think father is fit for company today. He‚Äôs lying there quite gray in the face‚ÅÝ‚Äîif he could only cry even! Oh, those two brothers have always been so much to each other till now! They were so united in everything!‚Äù

Pelle went; he was thinking earnestly. He could see that Stolpe, in his integrity, would consider it his duty to treat his brother more harshly than others, dearly as he loved him; perhaps he himself would undertake the picketing of the place where his brother went to work.

Out by the lakes he met a squad of pickets who were on their way out of the city; he accompanied them for some distance, in order to make certain arrangements. Across the road a young fellow came out of a doorway and slunk round the corner. ‚ÄúYou there, stop!‚Äù cried one of the comrades. ‚ÄúThere he is‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe toff!‚Äù A few pickets followed him down Castle Street and came back leading him among them. A crowd began to form round the whole party, women and children speedily joining it.

‚ÄúYou are not to do anything to him,‚Äù said Pelle decisively.

‚ÄúGod knows no one wants to touch him!‚Äù they retorted. For a while they stood silently gazing at him, as though weighing him in their minds; then one after another spat at him, and they went their way. The fellow went silently into a doorway and stood there wiping the spittle from his face with his sleeve. Pelle followed him in order to say a kind word to him and lead him back into the organization. The lad pulled himself up hastily as Pelle approached.

‚ÄúAre you coming to spit at me?‚Äù he said contemptuously. ‚ÄúYou forgot it before‚ÅÝ‚Äîwhy didn‚Äôt you do it then?‚Äù

‚ÄúI don‚Äôt spit at people,‚Äù said Pelle, ‚Äúbut your comrades are right to despise you. You have left them in the lurch. Come with me, and I‚Äôll enter you in the organization again, and no one shall molest you.‚Äù

‚ÄúI am to go about as a culprit and be taunted‚ÅÝ‚Äîno, thanks!‚Äù

‚ÄúDo you prefer to injure your own comrades?‚Äù

‚ÄúI ask for permission to look after my old mother. The rest of you can go to the devil. My mother isn‚Äôt going to hang about courtyards singing, and picking over the dustbins, while her son plays the great man! I leave that to certain other people!‚Äù

Pelle turned crimson. He knew this allusion was meant for Father Lasse; the desperate condition of the old man was lurking somewhere in his mind like an ingrowing grief, and now it came to the surface. ‚ÄúDare you repeat what you said?‚Äù he growled, pressing close up to the other.

‚ÄúAnd if I were married I shouldn‚Äôt let my wife earn my daily bread for me‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI should leave that to the pimps!‚Äù

Oho! That was like the tattlers, to blacken a man from behind! Evidently they were spreading all sorts of lying rumors about him, while he had placed all that he possessed at their disposal. Now Pelle was furious; the leader could go to hell! He gave the fellow a few sound boxes on the ear, and asked him which he would rather do‚ÅÝ‚Äîhold his mouth or take some more?

Morten appeared in the doorway‚ÅÝ‚Äîthis had happened in the doorway of the house in which he worked. ‚ÄúThis won‚Äôt do!‚Äù he whispered, and he drew Pelle away with him. Pelle could make no reply; he threw himself on Morten‚Äôs bed. His eyes were still blazing with anger at the insult, and he needed air.

‚ÄúThings are going badly here now,‚Äù said Morten, looking at him with a peculiar smile.

‚ÄúYes, I know very well you can‚Äôt stand it‚ÅÝ‚Äîall the same, they must hold together.‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd supposing they don‚Äôt get better conditions?‚Äù

‚ÄúThen they must accept the consequences. That‚Äôs better than the whole Cause should go to the wall!‚Äù

‚ÄúAre those the new ideas? I think the ignorant have always had to take the consequences! And there has never been lacking someone to spit on them!‚Äù said Morten sadly.

‚ÄúBut, listen!‚Äù cried Pelle, springing to his feet. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôll please not blame me for spitting at anybody‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe others did that!‚Äù He was very near losing his temper again, but Morten‚Äôs quiet manner mastered him.

‚ÄúThe others‚ÅÝ‚Äîthat was nothing at all! But it was you who spat seven times over into the poor devil‚Äôs face‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI was standing in the shop, and saw it.‚Äù

Pelle stared at him, speechless. Was this the truth-loving Morten who stood there lying?

‚ÄúYou say you saw me spit at him?‚Äù

Morten nodded. ‚ÄúDo you want to accept the applause and the honor, and sneak out of the beastliness and the destruction? You have taken a great responsibility on yourself, Pelle. Look, how blindly they follow you‚ÅÝ‚Äîat the sight of your bare face, I‚Äôm tempted to say. For I‚Äôm not myself quite sure that you give enough of yourself. There is blood on your hands‚ÅÝ‚Äîbut is any of it your own blood?‚Äù

Pelle sat there heavily pondering; Morten‚Äôs words always forced his thoughts to follow paths they had never before known. But now he understood him; and a dark shadow passed over his face, which left its traces behind it. ‚ÄúThis business has cost me my home,‚Äù he said quietly. ‚ÄúEllen cares nothing for me now, and my children are being neglected, and are drifting away from me. I have given up splendid prospects for the future; I go hungry every day, and I have to see my old father in want and wretchedness! I believe no one can feel as homeless and lonely and forsaken as I do! So it has cost me something‚ÅÝ‚Äîyou force me to say it myself.‚Äù He smiled at Morten, but there were tears in his eyes.

‚ÄúForgive me, my dear friend!‚Äù said Morten. ‚ÄúI was afraid you didn‚Äôt really know what you were doing. Already there are many left on the field of battle, and it‚Äôs grievous to see them‚ÅÝ‚Äîespecially if it should all lead to nothing.‚Äù

‚ÄúDo you condemn the Movement, then? According to you, I can never do anything wise!‚Äù

‚ÄúNot if it leads to an end! I myself have dreamed of leading them on to fortune‚ÅÝ‚Äîin my own way; but it isn‚Äôt a way after their own heart. You have power over them‚ÅÝ‚Äîthey follow you blindly‚ÅÝ‚Äîlead them on, then! But every wound they receive in battle should be yours as well‚ÅÝ‚Äîotherwise you are not the right man for the place. And are you certain of the goal?‚Äù

Yes, Pelle was certain of that. ‚ÄúAnd we are reaching it!‚Äù he cried, suddenly inspired. ‚ÄúSee how cheerfully they approve of everything, and just go forward!‚Äù

‚ÄúBut, Pelle!‚Äù said Morten, with a meaning smile, laying his hand on his shoulder, ‚Äúa leader is not Judge Lynch. Otherwise the parties would fight it out with clubs!‚Äù

‚ÄúAh, you are thinking of what happened just now!‚Äù said Pelle. ‚ÄúThat had nothing to do with the Movement! He said my father was going about the backyards fishing things out of dustbins‚ÅÝ‚Äîso I gave him a few on the jaw. I have the same right as anyone else to revenge an insult.‚Äù He did not mention the evil words concerning Ellen; he could not bring himself to do so.

‚ÄúBut that is true,‚Äù said Morten quietly.

‚ÄúThen why didn‚Äôt you tell me?‚Äù asked Pelle.

‚ÄúI thought you knew it. And you have enough to struggle against as it is‚ÅÝ‚Äîyou‚Äôve nothing to reproach yourself with.‚Äù

‚ÄúPerhaps you can tell me where he could be found?‚Äù said Pelle, in a low voice.

‚ÄúHe is usually to be found in this quarter.‚Äù

Pelle went. His mind was oppressed; all that day fresh responsibilities had heaped themselves upon him; a burden heavy for one man to bear. Was he to accept the responsibility for all that the Movement destroyed as it progressed, simply because he had placed all his energies and his whole fortune at its disposal? And now Father Lasse was going about as a scavenger. He blushed for shame‚ÅÝ‚Äîyet how could he have prevented it? Was he to be made responsible for the situation? And now they were spitting upon Ellen‚ÅÝ‚Äîthat was the thanks he got!

He did not know where to begin his search, so he went into the courts and backyards and asked at random. People were crowding into a courtyard in Blaagaard Street, so Pelle entered it. There was a missionary there who spoke with the singsong accent of the Bornholmer, in whose eyes was the peculiar expression which Pelle remembered as that of the ‚Äúsaints‚Äù of his childhood. He was preaching and singing alternately. Pelle gazed at him with eyes full of reminiscence, and in his despairing mood he was near losing control of himself and bellowing aloud as in his childish years when anything touched him deeply. This was the very lad who had said something rude about Father Lasse, and whom he‚ÅÝ‚Äîyoung as he was‚ÅÝ‚Äîhad kicked so that he became ruptured. He was able to protect his father in those days, at all events!

He went up to the preacher and held out his hand. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs Peter Kune! So you are here?‚Äù

The man looked at him with a gaze that seemed to belong to another world. ‚ÄúYes, I had to come over here, Pelle!‚Äù he said significantly. ‚ÄúI saw the poor wandering hither from the town and farther away, so I followed them, so that no harm should come to them. For you poor are the chosen people of God, who must wander and wander until they come into the Kingdom. Now the sea has stayed you here, and you can go no farther; so you think the Kingdom must lie here. God has sent me to tell you that you are mistaken. And you, Pelle, will you join us now? God is waiting and longing for you; he wants to use you for the good of all these little ones.‚Äù And he held Pelle‚Äôs hand in his, gazing at him compellingly; perhaps he thought Pelle had come in order to seek the shelter of his ‚ÄúKingdom.‚Äù

Here was another who had the intention of leading the poor to the land of fortune! But Pelle had his own poor. ‚ÄúI have done what I could for them,‚Äù he said self-consciously.

‚ÄúYes, I know that well; but that is not the right way, the way you are following! You do not give them the bread of life!‚Äù

‚ÄúI think they have more need of black bread. Look at them‚ÅÝ‚Äîd‚Äôyou think they get too much to eat?‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd can you give them food, then? I can give them the joy of God, so that they forget their hunger for a while. Can you do more than make them feel their hunger even more keenly?‚Äù

‚ÄúPerhaps I can. But I‚Äôve got no time to talk it over now; I came to look for my old father.‚Äù

‚ÄúYour father, I have met in the streets lately, with a sack on his back‚ÅÝ‚Äîhe did not look very cheerful. And I met him once over yonder with Sort the shoemaker; he wanted to come over here and spend his old age with his son.‚Äù

Pelle said nothing, but ran off. He clenched his fists in impotent wrath as he rushed out of the place. People went about jeering at him, one more eagerly than the other, and the naked truth was that he‚ÅÝ‚Äîyoung and strong and capable as he was in his calling‚ÅÝ‚Äîcould not look after his wife and children and his old father, even when he had regular work. Yes, so damnable were the conditions that a man in the prime of his youth could not follow the bidding of nature and found a family without plunging those that were dependent on him into want and misery! Curse it all, the entire system ought to be smashed! If he had power over it he would want to make the best use of it!

In Stone Street he heard a hoarse, quavering voice singing in the central courtyard of one of the houses. It was Father Lasse. The ragbag lay near him, with the hook stuck into it. He was clasping the book with one hand, while with the other he gesticulated toward the windows as he sang. The song made the people smile, and he tried to make it still more amusing by violent gestures which ill-suited his pitiful appearance.

It cut Pelle to the heart to see his wretched condition. He stepped into a doorway and waited until his father should have finished his song. At certain points in the course of the song Lasse took off his cap and smacked it against his head while he raised one leg in the air. He very nearly lost his equilibrium when he did this, and the street urchins who surrounded him pulled at his ragged coattails and pushed one another against him. Then he stood still, spoke to them in his quavering voice, and took up his song again.

‚ÄúO listen to my song, a tale of woe:
I came into the world as do so many:
My mother bore me in the street below,
And as for father, why, I hadn‚Äôt any!
Till now I‚Äôve faithfully her shame concealed:
I tell it now to make my song complete.
O drop a shilling down that I may eat,
For eat I must, or soon to Death I yield.

‚ÄúInto this world without deceit I came,
That‚Äôs why you see me wear no stockings now.
A poor old man who drudges anyhow,
I have a wealthy brother, more‚Äôs the shame.
But he and I are opposites in all;
While I rake muck he rakes his money up:
Much gold is his and many a jewelled cup,
And all he fancies, that is his at call.

‚ÄúMy brother, he has built a palace splendid,
And silver harness all his horses bear.
Full twenty crowns an hour he gets, I hear,
By twiddling thumbs and wishing day were ended!
Gold comes to him as dirt to Lasse, blast him!
And everywhere he turns there money lies.
‚ÄôTwill all be mine when once my brother dies‚ÅÝ‚Äî
If I but live‚ÅÝ‚Äîso help me to outlast him!

‚ÄúLuck tried to help me once, but not again!
Weary with toiling I was like to swoon.
When God let fall milk-porridge ‚Äôstead of rain!
And I, poor donkey, hadn‚Äôt brought a spoon!
Yes, Heaven had meant to help me, me accurst!
I saw my luck but couldn‚Äôt by it profit!
Quickly my brother made a banquet of it‚ÅÝ‚Äî
Ate my milk-porridge till he nearly burst!

‚ÄúWant bears the sceptre here on earth below,
And life is always grievous to the poor.
But God, who rules the world, and ought to know,
Says all will get their rights when life is o‚Äôer.
Therefore, good people, hear me for His sake‚ÅÝ‚Äî
A trifle for the poor man‚Äôs coffin give,
Wherein his final journey he must take;
Have mercy on my end while yet I live!

‚ÄúYet one thing God has given me‚ÅÝ‚Äîmy boy.
And children are the poor man‚Äôs wealth, I know.
O does he think of me, my only joy,
Who have no other treasure here below?
Long time have we been parted by mishap:
I‚Äôm tired of picking rags and sick of song;
God who sees all reward you all ere long:
O drop a trifle in poor Lasse‚Äôs cap!‚Äù

When Lasse had finished his song the people clapped and threw down coins wrapped in paper, and he went round picking them up. Then he took his sack on his back and stumped away, bent almost double, through the gateway.

‚ÄúFather!‚Äù cried Pelle desperately. ‚ÄúFather!‚Äù

Lasse stood up with a jerk and peered through the gateway with his feeble eyes. ‚ÄúIs that you, lad? Ach, it sounded like your voice when you were a child, when anyone was going to hurt you and you came to me for help.‚Äù The old man was trembling from head to foot. ‚ÄúAnd now I suppose you‚Äôve heard the whole thing and are ashamed of your old father?‚Äù He dared not look at his son.

‚ÄúFather, you must come home with me now‚ÅÝ‚Äîdo you hear?‚Äù said Pelle, as they entered the street together.

‚ÄúNo, that I can‚Äôt do! There‚Äôs not enough even for your own mouths‚ÅÝ‚Äîno, you must let me go my own way. I must look after myself‚ÅÝ‚Äîand I‚Äôm doing quite well.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou are to come home with me‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe children miss you, and Ellen asks after you day after day.‚Äù

‚ÄúYes, that would be very welcome.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ But I know what folks would think if I were to take the food out of your children‚Äôs mouths! Besides‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI‚Äôm a ragpicker now! No, you mustn‚Äôt lead me into temptation.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou are to come with me now‚ÅÝ‚Äînever mind about anything else. I can‚Äôt bear this, father!‚Äù

‚ÄúWell, then, in God‚Äôs name, I must publish my shame before you, lad‚ÅÝ‚Äîif you won‚Äôt let me be! See now, I‚Äôm living with someone‚ÅÝ‚Äîwith a woman. I met her out on the refuse-heaps, where she was collecting rubbish, just as I was. I had arranged a corner for myself out there‚ÅÝ‚Äîfor the night, until I could find a lodging‚ÅÝ‚Äîand then she said I was to go home with her‚ÅÝ‚Äîit wouldn‚Äôt be so cold if there were two of us. Won‚Äôt you come home with me, so that you can see where we‚Äôve both got to? Then you can see the whole thing and judge for yourself. We live quite close.‚Äù

They turned into a narrow lane and entered a gateway. In the backyard, in a shed, which looked like the remains of an old farm cottage, was Lasse‚Äôs home. It looked as though it had once been used as a fuel-shed; the floor was of beaten earth and the roof consisted of loose boards. Under the roof cords were stretched, on which rags, paper, and other articles from the dustbins were hung to dry. In one corner was a mean-looking iron stove, on which a coffeepot was singing, mingling its pleasant fragrance with the musty stench of the rubbish. Lasse stretched himself to ease his limbs.

‚ÄúAch, I‚Äôm quite stiff!‚Äù he said, ‚Äúand a little chilled. Well, here you see my little mother‚ÅÝ‚Äîand this is my son, Pelle, my boy.‚Äù He contentedly stroked the cheeks of his new life‚Äôs partner.

This was an old, bent, withered woman, grimy and ragged; her face was covered with a red eruption which she had probably contracted on the refuse-heaps. But a pair of kind eyes looked out of it, which made up for everything else.

‚ÄúSo that is Pelle!‚Äù she said, looking at him. ‚ÄúSo that‚Äôs what he is like! Yes, one has heard his name; he‚Äôs one of those who will astonish the world, although he hasn‚Äôt red hair.‚Äù

Pelle had to drink a cup of coffee. ‚ÄúYou can only have bread-and-butter with it; we old folks can‚Äôt manage anything else for supper,‚Äù said Lasse. ‚ÄúWe go to bed early, both of us, and one sleeps badly with an overfull stomach.‚Äù

‚ÄúWell, now, what do you think of our home?‚Äù said Father Lasse, looking proudly about him. ‚ÄúWe pay only four kroner a month for it, and all the furniture we get for nothing‚ÅÝ‚Äîmother and I have brought it all here from the refuse-heaps, every stick of it, even the stove. Just look at this straw mattress, now‚ÅÝ‚Äîit‚Äôs really not bad, but the rich folks threw it away! And the iron bedstead‚ÅÝ‚Äîwe found that there; I‚Äôve tied a leg to it. And yesterday mother came in carrying those curtains, and hung them up. A good thing there are people who have so much that they have to throw it on the dust-heap!‚Äù

Lasse was quite cheerful; things seemed to be going well with him; and the old woman looked after him as if he had been the love of her youth. She helped him off with his boots and on with his list slippers, then she brought a long pipe out of the corner, which she placed between his lips; he smiled, and settled down to enjoy himself.

‚ÄúDo you see this pipe, Pelle? Mother saved up for this, without my knowing anything about it‚ÅÝ‚Äîshe has got such a long one I can‚Äôt light it myself! She says I look like a regular pope!‚Äù Lasse had to lean back in his chair while she lit the pipe.

When Pelle left, Lasse accompanied him across the yard. ‚ÄúWell, what do you think of it?‚Äù he said.

‚ÄúI am glad to see things are going so well with you,‚Äù said Pelle humbly.

Lasse pressed his hand. ‚ÄúThanks for that! I was afraid you would be strict about it. As quite a little boy, you used to be deucedly strict in that direction. And see now, of course, we could marry‚ÅÝ‚Äîthere is no impediment in either case. But that costs money‚ÅÝ‚Äîand the times are hard. As for children coming, and asking to be brought into the world respectably, there‚Äôs no danger of that.‚Äù

Pelle could not help smiling; the old man was so much in earnest.

‚ÄúLook in on us again soon‚ÅÝ‚Äîyou are always welcome,‚Äù said Lasse. ‚ÄúBut you needn‚Äôt say anything of this to Ellen‚ÅÝ‚Äîshe is so peculiar in that respect!‚Äù
